question:
15. Which is greater
\[
(\sqrt{n})^{\sqrt{n+1}} \text { or }(\sqrt{n+1})^{\sqrt{n}}
\]
where \( n>8 \) ?
solution:
Solution. \( (\sqrt{n})^{\sqrt{n+1}} \) is greater than \( (\sqrt{n+1})^{\sqrt{n}} \) for \( n>8 \). Consider the function \( f(x)=(\log x) / x \) for \( x>0 \). Its derivative is \( (1-\log x) / x^{2} \) which is negative for \( x>e \).

Hence, if \( e \leq x<y \) we have \( f(x)>f(y) \), and
\[
x y\left(\frac{\log x}{x}\right)>x y\left(\frac{\log y}{y}\right) .
\]

Taking exponentials we get
\[
\boldsymbol{e}^{y \log x}>\boldsymbol{e}^{x \log y},
\]
that is,
\[
x^{y}>y^{x}
\]
provided \( e \leq x<y \).
If \( n \geq 8 \), then \( e<\sqrt{n}<\sqrt{n+1} \), so
\[
(\sqrt{n})^{\sqrt{n+1}}>(\sqrt{n+1})^{v_{n}} .
\]

Remark. A number of interesting problems are based on the inequality (1). See for example Journal of Recreational Mathematics, vol. 2, no. 4 (October 1969), pages 255-256, where the inequality \( e^{\pi}>\pi^{e} \) and some generalizations are discussed. The inequality is related to Problem P.M. 1 of Competition 21 and Problem A.M. 1 of Competition 22.

descriptive_long.question:
15. Which is greater
\[
(\sqrt{indexn})^{\sqrt{indexn+1}} \text { or }(\sqrt{indexn+1})^{\sqrt{indexn}}
\]
where \( indexn>8 \) ?
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. \( (\sqrt{indexn})^{\sqrt{indexn+1}} \) is greater than \( (\sqrt{indexn+1})^{\sqrt{indexn}} \) for \( indexn>8 \). Consider the function \( funcfx(inputx)=(\log inputx) / inputx \) for \( inputx>0 \). Its derivative is \( (1-\log inputx) / inputx^{2} \) which is negative for \( inputx>e \).

Hence, if \( e \leq inputx<inputy \) we have \( funcfx(inputx)>funcfx(inputy) \), and
\[
inputx inputy\left(\frac{\log inputx}{inputx}\right)>inputx inputy\left(\frac{\log inputy}{inputy}\right) .
\]

Taking exponentials we get
\[
\boldsymbol{e}^{inputy \log inputx}>\boldsymbol{e}^{inputx \log inputy},
\]
that is,
\[
inputx^{inputy}>inputy^{inputx}
\]
provided \( e \leq inputx<inputy \).
If \( indexn \geq 8 \), then \( e<\sqrt{indexn}<\sqrt{indexn+1} \), so
\[
(\sqrt{indexn})^{\sqrt{indexn+1}}>(\sqrt{indexn+1})^{vindexn} .
\]

Remark. A number of interesting problems are based on the inequality (1). See for example Journal of Recreational Mathematics, vol. 2, no. 4 (October 1969), pages 255-256, where the inequality \( e^{\pi}>\pi^{e} \) and some generalizations are discussed. The inequality is related to Problem P.M. 1 of Competition 21 and Problem A.M. 1 of Competition 22.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
15. Which is greater
\[
(\sqrt{zephyrion})^{\sqrt{zephyrion+1}} \text { or }(\sqrt{zephyrion+1})^{\sqrt{zephyrion}}
\]
where \( zephyrion>8 \) ?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. \( (\sqrt{zephyrion})^{\sqrt{zephyrion+1}} \) is greater than \( (\sqrt{zephyrion+1})^{\sqrt{zephyrion}} \) for \( zephyrion>8 \). Consider the function \( nebulance(pandorium)=(\log pandorium) / pandorium \) for \( pandorium>0 \). Its derivative is \( (1-\log pandorium) / pandorium^{2} \) which is negative for \( pandorium>e \).

Hence, if \( e \leq pandorium<quasarine \) we have \( nebulance(pandorium)>nebulance(quasarine) \), and
\[
pandorium\, quasarine\left(\frac{\log pandorium}{pandorium}\right)>pandorium\, quasarine\left(\frac{\log quasarine}{quasarine}\right) .
\]

Taking exponentials we get
\[
\boldsymbol{e}^{quasarine \log pandorium}>\boldsymbol{e}^{pandorium \log quasarine},
\]
that is,
\[
pandorium^{quasarine}>quasarine^{pandorium}
\]
provided \( e \leq pandorium<quasarine \).
If \( zephyrion \geq 8 \), then \( e<\sqrt{zephyrion}<\sqrt{zephyrion+1} \), so
\[
(\sqrt{zephyrion})^{\sqrt{zephyrion+1}}>(\sqrt{zephyrion+1})^{astronauta} .
\]

Remark. A number of interesting problems are based on the inequality (1). See for example Journal of Recreational Mathematics, vol. 2, no. 4 (October 1969), pages 255-256, where the inequality \( e^{\pi}>\pi^{e} \) and some generalizations are discussed. The inequality is related to Problem P.M. 1 of Competition 21 and Problem A.M. 1 of Competition 22.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
15. Which is greater
\[
(\sqrt{endlesscount})^{\sqrt{endlesscount+1}} \text { or }(\sqrt{endlesscount+1})^{\sqrt{endlesscount}}
\]
where \( endlesscount>8 \) ?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. \( (\sqrt{endlesscount})^{\sqrt{endlesscount+1}} \) is greater than \( (\sqrt{endlesscount+1})^{\sqrt{endlesscount}} \) for \( endlesscount>8 \). Consider the function \( staticentity(steadyvalue)=(\log steadyvalue) / steadyvalue \) for \( steadyvalue>0 \). Its derivative is \( (1-\log steadyvalue) / steadyvalue^{2} \) which is negative for \( steadyvalue>e \).

Hence, if \( e \leq steadyvalue<lesserpoint \) we have \( staticentity(steadyvalue)>staticentity(lesserpoint) \), and
\[
steadyvalue lesserpoint\left(\frac{\log steadyvalue}{steadyvalue}\right)>steadyvalue lesserpoint\left(\frac{\log lesserpoint}{lesserpoint}\right) .
\]

Taking exponentials we get
\[
\boldsymbol{e}^{lesserpoint \log steadyvalue}>\boldsymbol{e}^{steadyvalue \log lesserpoint},
\]
that is,
\[
steadyvalue^{lesserpoint}>lesserpoint^{steadyvalue}
\]
provided \( e \leq steadyvalue<lesserpoint \).
If \( endlesscount \geq 8 \), then \( e<\sqrt{endlesscount}<\sqrt{endlesscount+1} \), so
\[
(\sqrt{endlesscount})^{\sqrt{endlesscount+1}}>(\sqrt{endlesscount+1})^{firmscalar} .
\]

Remark. A number of interesting problems are based on the inequality (1). See for example Journal of Recreational Mathematics, vol. 2, no. 4 (October 1969), pages 255-256, where the inequality \( e^{\pi}>\pi^{e} \) and some generalizations are discussed. The inequality is related to Problem P.M. 1 of Competition 21 and Problem A.M. 1 of Competition 22.

garbled_string.question:
15. Which is greater
\[
(\sqrt{qzxwvtnp})^{\sqrt{qzxwvtnp+1}} \text { or }(\sqrt{qzxwvtnp+1})^{\sqrt{qzxwvtnp}}
\]
where \( qzxwvtnp>8 \) ?
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. \( (\sqrt{qzxwvtnp})^{\sqrt{qzxwvtnp+1}} \) is greater than \( (\sqrt{qzxwvtnp+1})^{\sqrt{qzxwvtnp}} \) for \( qzxwvtnp>8 \). Consider the function \( zlxksmpt(hjgrksla)=(\log hjgrksla) / hjgrksla \) for \( hjgrksla>0 \). Its derivative is \( (1-\log hjgrksla) / hjgrksla^{2} \) which is negative for \( hjgrksla>e \).

Hence, if \( e \leq hjgrksla<nmbcvlqe \) we have \( zlxksmpt(hjgrksla)>zlxksmpt(nmbcvlqe) \), and
\[
hjgrksla nmbcvlqe\left(\frac{\log hjgrksla}{hjgrksla}\right)>hjgrksla nmbcvlqe\left(\frac{\log nmbcvlqe}{nmbcvlqe}\right) .
\]

Taking exponentials we get
\[
\boldsymbol{e}^{nmbcvlqe \log hjgrksla}>\boldsymbol{e}^{hjgrksla \log nmbcvlqe},
\]
that is,
\[
hjgrksla^{nmbcvlqe}>nmbcvlqe^{hjgrksla}
\]
provided \( e \leq hjgrksla<nmbcvlqe \).
If \( qzxwvtnp \geq 8 \), then \( e<\sqrt{qzxwvtnp}<\sqrt{qzxwvtnp+1} \), so
\[
(\sqrt{qzxwvtnp})^{\sqrt{qzxwvtnp+1}}>(\sqrt{qzxwvtnp+1})^{bgtrplsw} .
\]

Remark. A number of interesting problems are based on the inequality (1). See for example Journal of Recreational Mathematics, vol. 2, no. 4 (October 1969), pages 255-256, where the inequality \( e^{\pi}>\pi^{e} \) and some generalizations are discussed. The inequality is related to Problem P.M. 1 of Competition 21 and Problem A.M. 1 of Competition 22.

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer  
  m \geq  2.  
For every integer  
  n \geq  N(m) := \lceil e^{\,m+1}\rceil   
consider the two positive numbers  

  A_{n,m}=\prod _{j=0}^{m} (n+j)^{\,(n+j+1)^{\,1/(m+1)}},  

  B_{n,m}=\prod _{j=0}^{m} (n+j+1)^{\,(n+j)^{\,1/(m+1)}}.  

Determine which of the two numbers A_{n,m} and B_{n,m} is larger.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout the proof put  

 c := 1/(m+1)  (so 0 < c \leq  1/3 because m \geq  2).                        (0)

Step 1. Reduce the comparison to logarithms.  
Define  

 \Delta _{n,m} := log A_{n,m} - log B_{n,m}.  

A direct expansion gives  

 \Delta _{n,m}=\Sigma _{j=0}^{m}\!\Bigl[(n+j+1)^{c}\log(n+j)-(n+j)^{c}\log(n+j+1)\Bigr].  (1)

Thus A_{n,m}>B_{n,m} \Leftrightarrow  \Delta _{n,m}>0.

Step 2. Introduce a decreasing auxiliary function.  
Let  

 f(x):= log x / x^{c},  x>0.                                            (2)

Its derivative is  

 f '(x)=x^{-1-c}(1-c log x).                                           (3)

Because c = 1/(m+1), we have 1-c log x<0 precisely when log x>1/c = m+1.  
Hence f is strictly decreasing on (e^{\,m+1},\infty ).

Step 3. A point-wise inequality.  
For every x>e^{\,m+1}, monotonicity gives  

 f(x) > f(x+1).                                                        (4)

Multiplying both sides of (4) by the positive factor x^{c}(x+1)^{c} yields  

 (x+1)^{c}\log x > x^{c}\log(x+1).                                    (5)

Define  

 g(x):=(x+1)^{c}\log x-x^{c}\log(x+1).                                (6)

Then g(x)>0 for every x>e^{\,m+1}.

Step 4. Apply the point-wise result to every summand.  
Because n \geq  \lceil e^{\,m+1}\rceil  and e^{\,m+1} is irrational (and thus not an integer), we actually have the strict inequality n>e^{\,m+1}.  
Consequently n+j>e^{\,m+1} for every 0\leq j\leq m, so by (6)

 g(n+j)>0 for all j.                                                   (7)

But g(n+j) is exactly the j-th term in (1). Hence every summand of \Delta _{n,m} is positive, and

 \Delta _{n,m}=\Sigma _{j=0}^{m}g(n+j)>0.                                         (8)

Step 5. Conclusion.  
Inequality (8) implies log A_{n,m}>log B_{n,m}; exponentiating gives

 A_{n,m}>B_{n,m} for every integer n \geq  N(m)=\lceil e^{\,m+1}\rceil .              (9)

Therefore the required comparison is settled:

  boxed{\,A_{n,m}\;>\;B_{n,m}\text{ for all integers }n\ge\lceil e^{\,m+1}\rceil,\,m\ge2.\,}

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher dimension / more variables  
   • The original compares two simple numbers, the new problem involves an arbitrary integer parameter $m$ and a product of $m+1$ intertwined factors.  
   • The threshold $N(m)$ must be located explicitly in terms of $m$.

2. Additional constraints  
   • The comparison must hold uniformly for all $n\ge N(m)$ and all fixed $m\ge2$, not merely for large $n$ in one special case.

3. More sophisticated structures  
   • The proof requires analysing a function $f(x)=\log x/x^{1/(m+1)}$, establishing monotonicity via calculus, and then converting that analytic property into a combinatorial–symmetry argument over $m+1$ indices.

4. Deeper theoretical requirements  
   • One must manage decreasing functions on unbounded domains, determine precise regions of monotonicity, and handle delicate pairwise cancellations in a non-trivial sum.

5. Multiple interacting concepts  
   • Calculus (derivatives and monotonicity), exponential–logarithmic inequalities, index symmetry, and careful bounding all interact.  
   • The solution scales with $m$, demanding an argument that works in every dimension rather than a one-off trick.

All these layers make the enhanced variant markedly harder than both the original problem and the existing kernel variant, which dealt with a single fixed fractional power and required only a one-line monotonicity observation.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer  
  m \geq  2.  
For every integer  
  n \geq  N(m) := \lceil e^{\,m+1}\rceil

 \Delta _{n,m}=\Sigma _{j=0}^{m}g(n+j)>0.                                         (8)

Step 5. Conclusion.  
Inequality (8) implies log A_{n,m}>log B_{n,m}; exponentiating gives

 A_{n,m}>B_{n,m} for every integer n \geq  N(m)=\lceil e^{\,m+1}\rceil .              (9)

Therefore the required comparison is settled:

  boxed{\,A_{n,m}\;>\;B_{n,m}\text{ for all integers }n\ge\lceil e^{\,m+1}\rceil,\,m\ge2.\,}
